question: Based on this image, which diagnosis is most likely?
ground_truth_reasoning:
First, I examine the sagittal contrast-enhanced CT image. I see a focal, contrast-filled outpouching arising from the anterior aspect of the thoracic aorta (marked by the arrow), which communicates with the aortic lumen and has an irregular margin.

Next, I relate these imaging findings to the diagnostic question. The key imaging feature is a contained, enhancing sac directly contiguous with the aorta rather than a separate solid mass in the mediastinum.

Then I consider how each option fits the image. The appearance of a discrete contrast-filled sac connected to the aortic lumen favors an aortic pseudoaneurysm (option A). A true saccular aortic aneurysm would more likely show a focal dilation with intact aortic wall architecture and a broad-based neck; the current image shows a more focal, irregular outpouching consistent with a contained disruption. An aortic dissection would typically show an intimal flap and a double lumen within the aortic lumen, which are not seen here. A mediastinal neoplasm would generally appear as a non-vascular soft-tissue mass that does not directly fill with intravascular contrast from the aortic lumen.

In summary, the image demonstrates a focal contrast-filled sac communicating with the aorta and lacking imaging features of dissection or a separate mediastinal mass, which most closely matches an aortic pseudoaneurysm.
caption: (A-B) Repeat CT angiogram of the chest at the time of re-presentation, 11 days after the original study. Coronal (Fig. 3A) and sagittal (Fig. 3B) sequences demonstrate grossly similar opacification the pseudoaneurysm (arrow), but now with increased soft tissue nodularity, and increased opacifications throughout the lung fields.
sub_caption: (Fig. 3B) Repeat CT angiogram of the chest at the time of re-presentation, 11 days after the original study. Sagittal sequence demonstrates grossly similar opacification the pseudoaneurysm (arrow), but now with increased soft tissue nodularity, and increased opacifications throughout the lung fields.
image_context: The patient's hemoptysis improved, and she was extubated the following day. However, her clinical course was complicated by septic shock related to her CF exacerbation. Sputum cultures grew Burkholderia cepacian complex, precluding lung transplantation. Eventually, she was weaned off vasopressors and transferred out of the MICU, remaining hemoptysis-free for 11 days. An additional isolated hemoptysis recurrence prompted transfer back to the MICU. A repeat CTA at that time did not show any active extravasation, although it did show continued enhancement of the pseudoaneurysm in the left lung apex (<xref rid="gr3" ref-type="fig">Fig. 3</xref>A-B). Following a goals of care discussion, the multi-disciplinary decision was made to actively monitor, while having a low threshold to proceed with additional emergency embolization if the patient decompensated versus elective embolization. One week later the patient developed recurrent massive hemoptysis requiring intubation. Repeat CTA redemonstrated the 8-9 mm pseudoaneurysm in the medial left upper lobe without evidence of active extravasation, but with altered morphology compared to the most recent prior CTA. The decision was made to proceed with repeat embolization.A-B). Following a goals of care discussion, the multi-disciplinary decision was made to actively monitor, while having a low threshold to proceed with additional emergency embolization if the patient decompensated versus elective embolization. One week later the patient developed recurrent massive hemoptysis requiring intubation. Repeat CTA redemonstrated the 8-9 mm pseudoaneurysm in the medial left upper lobe without evidence of active extravasation, but with altered morphology compared to the most recent prior CTA. The decision was made to proceed with repeat embolization.Fig. 3(A-B) Repeat CT angiogram of the chest at the time of re-presentation, 11 days after the original study. Coronal (<xref rid="gr3" ref-type="fig">Fig. 3</xref>A) and sagittal (A) and sagittal (<xref rid="gr3" ref-type="fig">Fig. 3</xref>B) sequences demonstrate grossly similar opacification the pseudoaneurysm (arrow), but now with increased soft tissue nodularity, and increased opacifications throughout the lung fields.B) sequences demonstrate grossly similar opacification the pseudoaneurysm (arrow), but now with increased soft tissue nodularity, and increased opacifications throughout the lung fields.Fig 3
relevant_image_context: Fig. 3(A-B) Repeat CT angiogram of the chest at the time of re-presentation, 11 days after the original study. Coronal (Fig. 3 A) and sagittal (A) and sagittal (Fig. 3 B) sequences demonstrate grossly similar opacification of the pseudoaneurysm (arrow), but now with increased soft tissue nodularity, and increased opacifications throughout the lung fields.
